hypothesis: If we surface a contextual in-app prompt nudging users to complete onboarding step 5 — shown at the moment they finish step 4 and again via a 24-hour follow-up email if step 5 remains incomplete — then a greater proportion of newly signed-up users will fire the `onboarding_step_completed` (step_index=5) event, leading to a measurable increase in week-4 retention compared to the control group.
target_segment: Users who have completed onboarding steps 1–4 (fired `onboarding_step_completed` with step_index=4) but have NOT yet completed step 5, within their first 7 days post-signup.
guardrail_metrics: post-nudge 30-day churn rate (watch for forced completion with no genuine engagement)
support contact rate within 7 days of nudge (watch for confusion or frustration caused by the prompt)
plan downgrade rate within 30 days (watch for over-prompted users disengaging from paid tiers)
expected_effect_size: +7.0 percentage points in week-4 retention rate (approximately 14% of the observed +49.5pp retention gap, representing a realistic causal slice after accounting for selection effects)
rationale:
Onboarding step 5 completion is one of the strongest retention signals in this dataset: users who reach it retain at 70.4% versus 20.9% for those who don't — a +49.5pp gap and a 3.37x lift. The cohort who completes step 5 (n=6,188, 24.8% of total) skews heavily toward high-intent personas: 60.3% are "power" users (≈85% baseline retention) and 39.7% are "activators" (≈50% baseline retention). This composition strongly suggests that a significant portion of the observed gap reflects pre-existing user motivation rather than a purely causal effect of step 5 itself. Consequently, we conservatively estimate that a well-designed nudge will convert roughly 14% of the correlation gap into true causal lift — yielding approximately +7.0pp in week-4 retention — rather than anything approaching the full +49.5pp.

The intervention is a two-touch nudge: (1) an in-app modal or progress-bar prompt that fires immediately after `onboarding_step_completed` with step_index=4, surfacing a clear, one-click path to step 5 with a brief value-framing message tied to the specific action step 5 requires; and (2) a single triggered email sent 24 hours later if step 5 remains incomplete, containing a deep link directly to the step. This design is minimally disruptive (only users who are one step away are targeted), avoids overwhelming users who are already progressing naturally, and keeps the nudge anchored to demonstrated momentum — reducing the risk of wasted impressions on truly disengaged "looker" or "bouncer" users who have already dropped off before step 4.

hypothesis: If users in their first 14 days post-signup who have not yet connected an integration are shown a persistent, contextual in-app prompt (surfaced on the project dashboard) highlighting the specific integrations available — Slack, GitHub, Google Drive, Jira — with one-click setup flows, then a meaningful fraction will connect at least one integration within 14 days of signup, increasing their week-4 retention rate by approximately 7–10 percentage points compared to a control group receiving no prompt.
target_segment: Users in days 1–13 post-signup who have completed at least one onboarding step (i.e., are past the bouncer stage) but have not yet fired an `integration_connected` event — across free, pro, and business plan tiers.
guardrail_metrics: week-4 churn rate among nudged users (regression indicator if prompt causes friction or false-start integrations that break workflows)
support contact rate within 7 days of integration prompt shown (spike may indicate confusing UX or broken integration setup flows)
plan downgrade rate within 30 days (early monetization signal that prompt is not driving durable value)
expected_effect_size: +8.0 percentage points in week-4 retention rate (~17% of the observed +47.5pp correlation gap, consistent with the realistic causal range of +4.8pp to +14.3pp)
rationale:
The `integration_connected_within_14_days` milestone shows a stark retention gap: users who hit it retain at 68.8% versus 21.2% for those who don't — a +47.5pp difference across a cohort of 6,248 users (25% of the total population). The cohort's persona composition reinforces this signal's strength: 57.0% are "power" users and 41.6% are "activators," meaning virtually no low-intent users (lookers: 1.4%, bouncers: effectively 0%) self-select into this milestone. This composition also explains why a naive read of the 3.24x lift overstates the causal story — motivated users disproportionately both seek out integrations and retain regardless. A realistic intervention effect must discount heavily for this self-selection.

The proposed intervention is a persistent suggestion card on the main project dashboard, appearing from day 1 through day 13 post-signup for eligible users. The card surfaces the four available integrations (Slack, GitHub, Google Drive, Jira) with a one-click OAuth setup flow directly inline — no navigation away from the current context. A secondary email nudge fires on day 5 if the card has been dismissed without action. By reducing the discovery and setup friction for a behavior already strongly correlated with retention, and by catching users while they are still actively exploring the product, we estimate a realistic causal lift of approximately +8.0pp in week-4 retention — roughly 17% of the observed correlation gap, within the conservative +4.8pp to +14.3pp plausible causal range.

hypothesis: If users in their first 7 days post-signup who have not yet connected an integration are shown a contextual in-app prompt (a persistent suggestion card in the sidebar + a triggered email nudge at 48 hours post-signup) surfacing the top 4 integrations (Slack, GitHub, Google Drive, Jira) with a one-click connect flow, then a greater proportion of them will fire an `integration_connected` event within 7 days, resulting in a measurable increase in week-4 retention.
target_segment: Users in their first 7 days post-signup who have completed at least one onboarding step (i.e., created a workspace or project) but have not yet fired an `integration_connected` event — representing the ~80.3% of the total population that did not reach the milestone organically.
guardrail_metrics: week-4 churn rate among nudged users (watch for force-connecting irrelevant integrations leading to faster disengagement)
support contact rate within 7 days of nudge delivery (watch for confusion or friction in the connect flow)
plan downgrade rate within 30 days (watch for users who connect but fail to find value, signaling shallow activation)
expected_effect_size: +7 to +10 percentage points increase in week-4 retention rate among the treated segment (representing roughly 15–20% of the observed +50.8pp retention gap, consistent with a realistic causal fraction after accounting for pre-existing intent and self-selection in the milestone cohort).
rationale:
The `integration_connected_within_7_days` milestone shows a striking retention signal: users who connect an integration within their first week retain at 73.9% versus 23.1% for those who don't — a +50.8pp gap across a cohort of 4,936 users (19.7% of the total population). Critically, the persona breakdown of the "did" cohort is heavily skewed toward high-intent users: 72.2% power users and 27.0% activators, with virtually no lookers or bouncers. This confirms that a large portion of the observed gap is driven by pre-existing engagement intent rather than the integration behavior itself, which is why we conservatively project a causal effect of +7 to +10pp rather than the full observed gap.

That said, even a modest causal nudge toward integration connection is worth pursuing because the action is a strong proxy for tool embeddedness — connecting Slack, GitHub, Jira, or Google Drive ties the product into a user's existing workflow, raising switching costs. The intervention targets the 80.3% of users who did not reach the milestone naturally, using a low-friction in-app suggestion card (persistent but dismissible) and a single 48-hour email nudge. By surfacing the one-click connect flow at a moment when users have already demonstrated some intent (having created a workspace or project), we increase the probability of activating users who are on the fence — the activator-profile users most likely to convert — without meaningfully disrupting already-engaged power users who will connect integrations on their own.

hypothesis: If users in their first 7 days post-signup who have completed fewer than 3 tasks receive a progressive in-app prompt sequence (a contextual task-completion checklist surfaced on Day 2 and a follow-up email nudge on Day 4 if still below threshold), then their rate of completing at least 3 tasks within Week 1 will increase, resulting in a measurable improvement in Week-4 retention relative to the control group.
target_segment: Users in days 1–7 post-signup who have fired fewer than 3 `task_completed` events and have at least 1 `task_created` event (i.e., they have started using tasks but have not yet crossed the retention-correlated completion threshold).
guardrail_metrics: Week-4 churn rate among nudged users (watch for increase vs. control, indicating the nudge drives hollow completions without real engagement)
Support contact rate within 7 days post-signup (watch for increase, indicating the prompts are confusing or disruptive)
Plan downgrade rate within 30 days post-signup (watch for increase among nudged users relative to control)
expected_effect_size: +7.5 percentage points in Week-4 retention rate (approximately 15% of the observed +50.2pp retention gap, reflecting a realistic causal fraction after discounting pre-existing user intent and selection effects).
rationale:
The milestone `task_completed_at_least_3_in_week1` is strongly associated with retention: users who hit it retain at 75.0% versus 24.8% for those who don't, a +50.2pp gap across a cohort of 4,161 users (16.6% of the total population). The persona breakdown reinforces that this milestone is dominated by naturally high-intent users — 72.8% power users and 27.2% activators — which means a large portion of the observed gap reflects selection bias rather than a purely causal relationship between task completion and retention. The users we are targeting (task-created but sub-threshold completers) sit closer to the activator profile, where a well-designed nudge has a realistic chance of shifting behavior.

Because the 3.03x lift is substantially inflated by self-selection, we conservatively estimate the causal effect of the intervention at roughly 15% of the raw gap, yielding a target of approximately +7.5pp in Week-4 retention. The intervention — a Day 2 in-app checklist prompt highlighting 3 tasks to complete, paired with a Day 4 email nudge for users still below threshold — is designed to be minimally disruptive while providing a concrete, achievable goal. Guardrail metrics focus on churn, support contacts, and downgrades to ensure that mechanically nudged completions do not mask disengagement or create friction that backfires downstream.

hypothesis: If users in their first 7 days post-signup who have created fewer than 3 tasks are shown a contextual in-app prompt (a "Quick Wins" task-creation card surfaced in an empty or sparse project view) encouraging them to add at least 3 tasks, then their week-4 retention rate will increase by approximately 7–10 percentage points compared to a control group that sees no such prompt.
target_segment: Users in days 1–6 post-signup who have fired fewer than 3 `task_created` events and have created at least one project (i.e., they are active enough to have a project context, but have not yet reached the 3-task milestone threshold).
guardrail_metrics: Week-4 churn rate among nudged users (watch for forced engagement leading to early drop-off)
Support contact rate within the first 14 days (watch for confusion or friction caused by the prompt)
Downgrade or plan-cancellation rate within 30 days (watch for users upgrading under pressure and then reverting)
expected_effect_size: +8 percentage points in week-4 retention rate (approximately 16% of the observed +49.8pp retention gap, reflecting a realistic causal fraction of the correlation after accounting for pre-existing user intent).
rationale:
The `task_created_at_least_3_in_week1` milestone shows a striking retention gap: users who hit it retain at 74.6% versus 24.9% for those who do not — a +49.8pp difference across a cohort of 4,142 users (16.6% of the total population). The milestone cohort skews heavily toward high-intent personas: 71.8% power users and 28.2% activators, both with above-average retention baselines. This means a substantial share of the observed gap reflects self-selection — motivated users naturally create more tasks and also happen to stick around. A prompt-based intervention cannot replicate that pre-existing intent, so a conservative causal estimate of ~16% of the raw gap (~+8pp) is appropriate.

The proposed intervention — a "Quick Wins" contextual card surfaced inside a project view when a user has fewer than 3 tasks after day 1 — is designed to reduce friction for users who are engaged enough to have created a project but have not yet formed a task-creation habit. Rather than a generic email nudge (which targets cold users), this in-product prompt meets users in an active work context, making it more likely to convert incremental task-creation behavior in the activator segment (28.2% of milestone achievers), where the retention payoff of crossing the 3-task threshold is most actionable. Power users in this segment will largely self-convert regardless; the experiment's real leverage is on the fence-sitters.

hypothesis: If users in their first 7 days post-signup who have created fewer than 3 projects are shown a contextual in-app prompt ("Project Quick-Start" suggestion cards surfaced after each project creation, highlighting the value of organizing work into multiple projects), then a greater proportion will reach the 3-project threshold within Week 1, resulting in a measurable increase in Week 4 retention rate compared to control.
target_segment: Users in days 1–7 post-signup who have created at least 1 but fewer than 3 projects, and have not yet triggered a third `project_created` event — i.e., users who have demonstrated initial intent but have not yet crossed the milestone threshold.
guardrail_metrics: Week 4 churn rate among nudged users (watch for superficial project creation without meaningful engagement)
Support contact rate within the first 14 days (watch for confusion or frustration caused by the prompts)
Downgrade rate from paid to free plan within 30 days (watch for users who feel pressured and disengage from paid tiers)
expected_effect_size: +7.5 percentage points in Week 4 retention rate (approximately 15% of the observed +49.0pp correlation gap, reflecting realistic causal attribution after accounting for pre-existing user intent)
rationale:
The `project_created_at_least_3_in_week1` milestone shows a striking retention gap: the 4,169 users (16.7% of the total population) who hit this threshold retain at 74.0%, versus only 24.9% for those who do not — a +49.0pp difference and a 2.97x lift. The cohort is heavily skewed toward high-intent personas: 71.9% are "power" users (expected ~85% retention) and 28.1% are "activator" users (expected ~50% retention), suggesting that a significant portion of the correlation is driven by pre-existing motivation rather than the project-creation behavior alone. However, the activator segment in particular represents users who are capable of sustained engagement but may need a structural nudge to discover the habit of multi-project organization.

Because much of the observed gap reflects self-selection, the intervention is designed conservatively. By targeting users who have already created 1–2 projects — demonstrating baseline intent — the experiment focuses on the marginal push needed to reach the third project, where habit formation appears to compound. The suggested prompt ("You've started 2 projects — teams that organize work into 3+ projects are significantly more likely to hit their goals") is grounded in social proof and mild urgency, without being coercive. A realistic causal effect of +7.5pp in Week 4 retention (roughly 15% of the observed +49.0pp gap) is plausible given the targeted segment's moderate engagement profile and the lightweight, non-disruptive nature of the intervention.

hypothesis: If we surface a contextual in-app prompt encouraging users to post comments on tasks during their first 7 days post-signup, then a measurable share of users who would not have reached the 3-comment threshold on their own will do so, increasing week-4 retention among the nudged group by approximately 7–10 percentage points.
target_segment: Users in days 1–6 post-signup who have posted fewer than 3 comments to date and have at least 1 task visible in their workspace (i.e., there is something to comment on), excluding users who have already hit the 3-comment milestone.
guardrail_metrics: week-4 churn rate among nudged users (watch for increase vs. control, which would indicate prompt fatigue or friction)
support contact rate within 7 days of nudge exposure (spike may signal confusion about the commenting feature)
plan downgrade rate within 30 days post-signup (regression could indicate hollow engagement that doesn't convert to paid retention)
expected_effect_size: ~+7.3 percentage points in week-4 retention rate (approximately 15% of the observed +48.8pp correlation gap, reflecting realistic causal conversion after adjusting for pre-existing intent in the milestone cohort)
rationale:
The `comment_posted_at_least_3_in_week1` milestone is one of the strongest behavioral signals in this dataset: users who hit it retain at 73.9% vs. 25.1% for those who don't — a +48.8pp gap and a 2.94x lift. However, only 16.4% of total users (4,107) reach this milestone organically, meaning the large majority never unlock its retention benefit. The cohort's persona composition (72% power users, 28% activators) confirms that milestone completers skew heavily toward already-engaged users, which means a substantial portion of the observed gap reflects selection bias rather than pure causal effect. A realistic intervention should therefore target the activator-like users who are present and creating tasks but not yet commenting — users who have latent intent but need a nudge to discover commenting as a collaboration habit.

The proposed intervention is a contextual, dismissible in-app prompt — surfaced on the task detail view after a user's first task is created — that highlights commenting as the primary way teammates stay aligned, and includes a one-click affordance to post a first comment immediately. A follow-up nudge (email or in-app) fires at day 3 if the user has fewer than 2 comments. Because the milestone requires only 3 comments in 7 days, the bar is achievable with a single session of intentional engagement. We estimate a causal lift of roughly +7.3pp (about 15% of the +48.8pp gap), which is conservative given the mix of power and activator personas in the did-cohort and the likelihood that nudged users skew toward activators with real but unactivated intent.

hypothesis: If users in their first 7 days post-signup who have completed fewer than 5 tasks receive a progressive in-app nudge sequence (a contextual prompt after task 1, a milestone progress card after task 3, and a congratulatory banner upon reaching task 5), then their week-4 retention rate will increase by approximately 8–10 percentage points compared to the control group who receives no such nudges.
target_segment: Users in days 1–7 post-signup who have fired at least 1 but fewer than 5 `task_completed` events and have not yet reached the `task_completed_at_least_5_in_week1` milestone threshold.
guardrail_metrics: Week-4 churn rate among nudged users (watch for anxiety-driven disengagement if prompts feel pushy)
Support contact rate within the first 14 days (a spike may indicate confusion or friction introduced by the nudge UI)
Downgrade rate from paid to free plan within 30 days post-signup (ensure nudges do not attract low-intent completions that later convert poorly)
expected_effect_size: +8 to +10 percentage points in week-4 retention rate (roughly 15–18% of the observed +54.1pp correlation gap, reflecting a realistic causal fraction after accounting for pre-existing user intent and selection effects)
rationale:
The observed data shows a stark retention gap: users who completed at least 5 tasks in week 1 retained at 83.5%, versus 29.3% for those who did not — a +54.1pp difference across a cohort of 1,754 users (7% of total). The persona breakdown further validates that this milestone is dominated by "power" users (96.1%), who exhibit ~85% natural retention, with only 3.9% "activator"-type users present. This composition tells us that the milestone is currently self-selected by the most motivated users; the causal lever available to an intervention is primarily to pull "activator"-profile users — who have moderate engagement potential (~50% retention) — across the 5-task threshold earlier in their lifecycle.

Because the majority of the retention signal reflects pre-existing high intent rather than a behavior that causally creates retention, we conservatively project a causal effect of +8 to +10pp — roughly 15–18% of the raw correlation gap. The intervention is a three-stage in-app nudge sequence: (1) a contextual tooltip after the first `task_completed` event surfacing a "5 tasks this week" progress goal, (2) a persistent progress card in the sidebar showing tasks completed vs. the 5-task target, and (3) a congratulatory in-app banner and optional team-share prompt upon hitting task 5. This sequence is designed to make the milestone legible and motivating without being coercive, targeting specifically the moderate-engagement users who are most movable.

hypothesis: If we surface a contextual in-app prompt encouraging users to invite at least one teammate during their first 7 days post-signup (before the 14-day milestone window closes), then we will increase the proportion of users who fire an `invite_sent` event within 14 days of signup, resulting in a measurable improvement in week-4 retention among the treated cohort.
target_segment: Users in days 3–7 post-signup who have created at least one project or task but have not yet sent any teammate invite — i.e., they have demonstrated enough product intent to benefit from collaboration but have not yet crossed the invite milestone.
guardrail_metrics: post-intervention 30-day churn rate (watch for forced-invite fatigue causing early cancellations among nudged users)
support contact rate within 7 days of nudge (watch for confusion or friction caused by the prompt)
downgrade rate from paid to free plan within 30 days (watch for invite friction discouraging plan retention)
expected_effect_size: +7pp to +10pp increase in week-4 retention rate among treated users (approximately 15–20% of the observed +48.0pp correlation gap, reflecting a realistic causal fraction after discounting pre-existing user intent)
rationale:
The `invite_sent_within_14_days` milestone shows a stark retention gap: the 3,515 users (14.1% of total) who hit this milestone retained at 74.4%, versus only 26.4% for those who did not — a +48.0pp gap and a 2.82x lift. The cohort composition strongly validates the milestone's signal quality: 80.9% of users who sent an invite within 14 days fall into the "power" persona (historically ~85% retention), while only 1.3% are "lookers." This suggests that invite-sending is both a strong marker of high-intent users and a plausible lever for activating moderate-intent ("activator") users, who represent 17.8% of the cohort.

However, the majority of the observed gap almost certainly reflects selection bias — motivated, collaborative users who were going to retain anyway are also the ones who naturally invite teammates. A realistic causal estimate for a nudge-based intervention is 15–20% of the raw gap, or roughly +7pp to +10pp in week-4 retention. The intervention is targeted at days 3–7, after a user has demonstrated enough product engagement (at least one project or task created) to make an invite contextually relevant, but early enough to act before the 14-day milestone window closes. The prompt should be low-friction — a suggestion card inside the product (e.g., "Your project is set up — get more done together. Invite a teammate →") rather than a modal blocker, to avoid alienating users who are working solo by design.

hypothesis: If users who have not yet sent a team invite within their first 7 days of signup are shown a contextual in-app prompt — surfaced immediately after their first project or task creation — encouraging them to invite a collaborator, then a meaningfully higher share will send at least one invite within 28 days of signup, increasing week-4 retention compared to the control group.
target_segment: Users in days 1–7 post-signup who have created at least one project or task but have not yet fired an `invite_sent` event — i.e., solo users who have demonstrated initial product intent but have not yet expanded to a collaborative workflow.
guardrail_metrics: Week-4 churn rate among nudged users (watch for annoyance-driven early drop-off if prompt cadence is too aggressive)
Support contact rate within 7 days of prompt exposure (proxy for user confusion or friction introduced by the invite flow)
Downgrade or plan-cancellation rate within 28 days (ensure invite nudge does not create expectation mismatch for solo-plan users)
expected_effect_size: +7 to +9 percentage points in week-4 retention rate (~15–20% of the observed +46.7pp correlation gap), reflecting a realistic causal uplift after accounting for the pre-existing intent that self-selects high-engagement users into the "did" cohort.
rationale:
The `invite_sent_within_28_days` milestone shows a striking retention gap: users who sent an invite retained at 72.3% versus 25.7% for those who did not — a +46.7pp difference across a cohort of 4,003 users (16% of the total population). The persona breakdown further validates the signal quality: 71.1% of the "did" cohort are power users and 27.0% are activators, with virtually no lookers or bouncers. This skew confirms that the milestone is a strong proxy for collaborative, high-intent usage — not merely a vanity action — making it a credible intervention target.

However, the raw correlation substantially overstates what a nudge can causally produce. Much of the 72.3% retention in the "did" cohort reflects pre-existing user motivation: power users and activators would likely have high retention regardless. A realistic intervention — a well-timed, non-intrusive in-app prompt shown after a user's first solo task or project creation — should convert a fraction of hesitant-but-capable users (likely sitting in the activator band) into collaborators, yielding an estimated +7 to +9pp causal lift. This sits comfortably within the plausible causal range of +4.7pp to +14.0pp and avoids overpromising on a correlation-driven number.
